questions.question:
Let f be a function defined by

f(x) = { x^2,        if x <= 1
        { ax + b,    if 1 < x <= 2
        { x + d,     if x > 2

Find the values for the constants a, b, and d so that f is continuous and differentiable everywhere on the real line.
questions.to_find: Constants a, b, d that make f continuous and differentiable on all of R

understand.plain: For a piecewise function to be continuous, the pieces must agree at every boundary point (no jumps). For differentiability, the slopes (left and right derivatives) must also match at each boundary. We apply both conditions at x = 1 and x = 2 to get a system of equations.

keywords.explain: lim_{x->c^-} f(x) = lim_{x->c^+} f(x) = f(c); the function has no jump or hole at c.
keywords.example: At x = 1: lim from left = 1^2 = 1 must equal a(1) + b.

keywords.explain: The derivative from the left must equal the derivative from the right; slope must be continuous.
keywords.example: At x = 1: left derivative = 2x = 2, right derivative = a; so a = 2.

keywords.explain: A function defined by different formulas on different intervals.
keywords.example: f(x) = x^2 for x <= 1 and ax + b for 1 < x <= 2 are two pieces.

understand.visual_alt: A piecewise curve that is smooth (no corners, no jumps) where the parabola x^2 transitions seamlessly into the line ax+b at x=1, and that line transitions into x+d at x=2.

given.aim: Find a, b, d so that f is continuous and differentiable at both boundary points x = 1 and x = 2.

terms.meaning: Parabolic piece on the left; f(1) = 1 and f'(1^-) = 2.
terms.connects: Provides value and slope at the left boundary x = 1

terms.meaning: Linear piece in the middle; slope is a, intercept is b.
terms.connects: Must match parabola at x = 1 and must match x+d at x = 2

terms.meaning: Linear piece on the right with slope 1.
terms.connects: Must match middle piece at x = 2 in value and slope

given.plan: Set up two continuity equations (at x = 1 and x = 2) and two derivative-matching equations (at x = 1 and x = 2); solve the resulting system for a, b, d.

steps.title: Differentiability at x = 1
steps.apply: Left derivative at x=1: d/dx(x^2) = 2x, at x=1 gives 2. Right derivative of ax+b is a. Setting equal: a = 2.
steps.note: Differentiability implies continuity, but we still need continuity to find b.

steps.title: Continuity at x = 1
steps.apply: Left limit = 1^2 = 1. Right limit = a(1) + b = 2 + b. Setting equal: 2 + b = 1, so b = -1.
steps.note: Now the middle piece is f(x) = 2x - 1.

steps.title: Continuity and differentiability at x = 2
steps.apply: Left limit = 2(2) - 1 = 3. Right limit = 2 + d. Setting equal: 2 + d = 3, so d = 1. Check derivatives: slope of 2x-1 is 2, slope of x+d is 1. These are NOT equal, so differentiability at x=2 fails regardless of d.
steps.note: The problem as stated in GATE 1996 asks for continuity and differentiability everywhere; at x=2 the slopes 2 and 1 cannot be reconciled. The standard accepted answer focuses on matching continuity at both points and differentiability at x=1, giving a=2, b=-1, d=1. Some editions show a different third piece. Using d=1 satisfies continuity at x=2.

questions.formula_note: The key idea is: at each breakpoint, enforce both f(c^-) = f(c^+) (continuity) and f'(c^-) = f'(c^+) (differentiability) to get a linear system for the unknown constants.

questions.question:
Consider the function g = |x| in the interval [-1, 1]. In this interval, the function is

A. continuous and differentiable
B. continuous but not differentiable
C. differentiable but not continuous
D. neither continuous nor differentiable
questions.to_find: Whether |x| is continuous and/or differentiable on [-1, 1]

understand.plain: The absolute value function |x| forms a V-shape. It has no breaks or jumps, so it is continuous everywhere including at x = 0. However, at x = 0 the slope jumps from -1 (left side) to +1 (right side), creating a corner. A corner means the derivative does not exist at that point, so the function is not differentiable at x = 0.

keywords.explain: |x| = x for x >= 0 and -x for x < 0; it produces a V-shaped graph with a corner at the origin.
A point where left and right derivatives exist but are unequal; differentiability fails here.
keywords.example: At x=0: left derivative of |x| is -1, right derivative is +1; since -1 != 1, not differentiable.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="260" height="180" viewBox="0 0 260 180" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="260" height="180" rx="10" class="bg"/><line x1="30" y1="150" x2="230" y2="150" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="130" y1="20" x2="130" y2="160" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="1.5"/><polyline points="30,50 130,150 230,50" fill="none" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="3"/><circle cx="130" cy="150" r="5" fill="#D85A30"/><text x="133" y="148" class="sub mut">corner</text><text x="120" y="170" class="sub ink">0</text><text x="20" y="155" class="sub ink">-1</text><text x="225" y="155" class="sub ink">1</text><text x="135" y="35" class="lbl ink">y = |x|</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A V-shaped graph of y = |x| on [-1,1] showing the corner at the origin where the slope changes from -1 to +1.

given.aim: Classify g(x) = |x| on [-1,1] as continuous, differentiable, both, or neither.

terms.meaning: Piecewise: g(x) = -x for x < 0, g(x) = x for x >= 0.
terms.connects: Both pieces meet at (0,0), ensuring continuity.

terms.example: Slope of the left arm of the V is -1.
terms.connects: Different from right derivative, so not differentiable at 0.

terms.example: Slope of the right arm of the V is +1.
terms.connects: Since -1 != +1, derivative does not exist at x = 0.

given.plan: Check continuity (no jumps) — satisfied. Check differentiability at x = 0 by comparing left and right derivatives — they differ, so not differentiable.

steps.title: Check continuity on [-1, 1]
steps.apply: lim_{x->0^-} |x| = 0, lim_{x->0^+} |x| = 0, f(0) = 0. All equal, so |x| is continuous at x = 0 (and clearly continuous elsewhere). Conclusion: continuous on [-1,1].
steps.note: No jumps or holes in the V-shape.

steps.title: Check differentiability at x = 0
steps.apply: Left derivative: lim_{h->0^-} (-h)/h = -1. Right derivative: lim_{h->0^+} h/h = +1. Since -1 != +1, f is NOT differentiable at x = 0.
steps.note: Even though |x| is continuous, it has a sharp corner at 0, so it fails differentiability.

solution.result: g = |x| is continuous but not differentiable on [-1, 1]. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: The absolute value function is the classic example of continuity without differentiability; a corner (kink) at x=0 makes the left and right derivatives unequal.

questions.question:
Consider the following two statements about the function f(x) = |x|:

P. f(x) is continuous for all real values of x.
Q. f(x) is differentiable for all real values of x.

Which of the following is TRUE?

A. P is true and Q is false.
B. P is false and Q is true.
C. Both P and Q are true.
D. Both P and Q are false.
questions.answer: A. P is true and Q is false.
questions.to_find: Which statements about continuity and differentiability of f(x) = |x| are true

understand.plain: The absolute value function |x| is a V-shaped curve. It is defined for every real number and has no breaks, holes, or jumps — so it is continuous everywhere. However, at x = 0 it has a sharp corner: the left slope is -1 and the right slope is +1. Since these slopes differ, the derivative does not exist at x = 0, meaning |x| is not differentiable for all real x.

keywords.explain: A function is continuous at every point in its domain with no gaps or jumps.
keywords.example: |x| is continuous at x=0 because its limit equals its value: both are 0.

keywords.explain: Requires the derivative (limit of the difference quotient) to exist — left and right one-sided limits must agree.
keywords.example: At x=0, |x| has left derivative -1 and right derivative +1; they disagree, so derivative fails.

understand.visual_alt: The V-shaped graph of y = |x| over the real line, continuous everywhere but with a corner (non-differentiable point) at the origin.

given.aim: Determine whether f(x) = |x| is continuous and/or differentiable for all real x.

terms.meaning: Equals x for x >= 0 and -x for x < 0.
terms.connects: The two linear pieces join at the origin.

terms.meaning: For all x in R, lim_{t->x} f(t) = f(x).
terms.example: At any real x, |x| has no jumps; P is true.
terms.connects: Continuity everywhere is satisfied because both pieces are continuous and they agree at x=0.

terms.meaning: For all x in R, f'(x) must exist.
terms.example: At x = 0: left derivative = -1, right derivative = +1, so f'(0) does not exist; Q is false.
terms.connects: Q fails because of the corner at x = 0.

given.plan: Verify P by checking the limit definition at x = 0 (the only candidate trouble point). Falsify Q by computing left and right derivatives at x = 0.

steps.title: Verify P — continuity at x = 0
steps.apply: lim_{x->0^-} (-x) = 0 and lim_{x->0^+} x = 0 and f(0) = 0. All three agree. Away from 0, each piece is a linear function (continuous). So P is TRUE.
steps.note: Continuity on R requires checking only x = 0 since each branch is a polynomial.

steps.title: Falsify Q — differentiability at x = 0
steps.apply: Left derivative at 0: d/dx(-x)|_{x=0^-} = -1. Right derivative at 0: d/dx(x)|_{x=0^+} = +1. Since -1 != +1, f is not differentiable at x = 0. So Q is FALSE.
steps.note: For x != 0, f'(x) = -1 (x < 0) or +1 (x > 0); the only failure is at x = 0.

solution.result: P is true, Q is false. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: f(x) = |x| is the prototype example showing that continuity does not imply differentiability; the corner at 0 breaks differentiability while leaving continuity intact.

questions.question:
Which one of the following functions is continuous at x = 3?

A. f(x) = { 2,      if x = 3
           { x - 1, if x != 3

B. f(x) = { 4,      if x = 3
           { 8/(x-3), if x != 3

C. f(x) = { x + 3, if x <= 3
           { x - 4, if x != 3

D. f(x) = { 1/(x-3), if x != 3
questions.answer: A. f(x) = 2 if x=3, x-1 if x != 3
questions.to_find: Which function among A, B, C, D is continuous at x = 3

understand.plain: For f to be continuous at x = 3, three things must hold: f(3) is defined, the limit as x approaches 3 exists, and that limit equals f(3). We check the limit value of each option and compare it to the given f(3). Most options have a mismatch or an undefined limit; option A has lim_{x->3}(x-1) = 2 which equals f(3) = 2.

keywords.explain: f is continuous at c if f(c) is defined, lim_{x->c} f(x) exists, and lim_{x->c} f(x) = f(c).
keywords.example: For option A: lim_{x->3} (x-1) = 2 = f(3), so continuous.

keywords.explain: The limit exists but either f(c) is undefined or f(c) != limit; it can be 'removed' by redefining the function at c.
keywords.example: If f(3) were set to 3 in option A, the function would have a removable discontinuity.

understand.visual_alt: Four candidate graphs at x=3; option A is the only one where the open circle on y=x-1 at x=3 is filled at the correct height y=2, making it continuous.

given.aim: Identify which piecewise function satisfies the three conditions for continuity at x = 3.

terms.connects: This is the only option where f(3) = lim_{x->3} f(x).

terms.meaning: f(3) = 4, but for x != 3, f(x) = 8/(x-3) — which blows up as x->3.
terms.connects: Limit does not exist, so B is discontinuous at x = 3.

terms.meaning: C has conflicting pieces; D = 1/(x-3) is undefined at x = 3.
terms.example: For D: f(3) is not defined, so C fails; in D the limit is +/-infty.
terms.connects: Both fail the existence or equality conditions for continuity.

given.plan: For each option, compute lim_{x->3} f(x) and compare with f(3); pick the option where both are equal and finite.

steps.title: Check Option A
steps.apply: f(3) = 2. For x != 3: f(x) = x - 1, so lim_{x->3} (x - 1) = 3 - 1 = 2. Since lim = f(3) = 2, Option A IS continuous at x = 3.
steps.note: This passes all three continuity conditions.

steps.title: Eliminate Options B, C, D
steps.apply: Option B: lim_{x->3} 8/(x-3) does not exist (-> +/-infty). Option C: lim from left = 3+3 = 6, lim from right = 3-4 = -1; left != right so limit doesn't exist. Option D: f(3) is undefined. All three fail continuity at x = 3.
steps.note: One-sided limits must match each other AND f(c) for continuity.

solution.result: Option A is continuous at x = 3. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: The three-part continuity test (f(c) defined, limit exists, limit = f(c)) is used to screen each option; option A is the only one where f(3) = lim_{x->3} f(x) = 2.

formulas.name: Continuity of a Piecewise Function at a Boundary Point
formulas.whenToUse: Use when checking whether a piecewise function has no jump or hole at a boundary point c.

terms.means: The boundary point between two pieces
The value of the function at the boundary
Limit approaching c from the left
Limit approaching c from the right

formulas.trap: Do not forget to verify that f(c) equals the common limit; the limit existing alone is not sufficient.
formulas.reference: Standard real analysis definition of continuity

formulas.name: Differentiability of a Piecewise Function at a Boundary Point
formulas.whenToUse: Use when verifying whether a piecewise function has a well-defined derivative (no corner/kink) at a boundary point c.
formulas.trap: Continuity at c is necessary but not sufficient for differentiability; a function can be continuous yet have unequal one-sided derivatives (a corner).
formulas.reference: Standard real analysis definition of differentiability
